snippet.title: Amazon Is “Heavily Making Investments” In ChatGPT Technology
snippet.description:
Amazon Is “Heavily Making Investments” In ChatGPT Technology

Amazon demands investors to know that it will not be left behind in the most recent arms race among Big Tech companies about artificial intelligence.

Using the same technology that powers ChatGPT and other comparable AI chatbots, Amazon (AMZN) CEO Andy Jassy wrote to shareholders on Thursday that the business is “investing heavily” in large language models (LLMs) and generative AI.
We have been making great efforts on our own LLMs for a while now, believe it will cause revolutionary changes and enhance virtually all types of the customer experiences, and will devote themselves to substantially invest in these models across all of our consumer, seller, brand, and creator experiences,” Jassy stated in his letter to shareholders.

The comments, which are a part of Jassy’s second annual shareholder letter since becoming CEO, allude to the pressure that many IT businesses experience in articulating how they can capitalize on the fast-developing market for AI solutions. Google (GOOG), Facebook (FB), and Microsoft (MSFT) have all talked about their increased focus on generative AI technology since ChatGPT was made available to the general public in late November. This technology can produce intriguing essays, stories, and pictures in response to user suggestions.
Jassy claims that Amazon wants to make machine learning chips more affordable so that “small and big multinational companies can afford to educate and head their LLMs in production.” For the purpose of producing responses to user prompts, large language models are trained on enormous amounts of data.
In an interview with CNBC on Thursday morning, Jassy stated that “most of the companies would like to utilize their language models, and the best ones use billions of dollars for training for multiple years. Most of these companies do not want to undergo these things.”

I think LLMs and generative AI will be so transformational that I could write a full letter about them, but I’ll save that for a later letter, Jassy said. “Let’s just say that LLMs and Generative AI is about to make big deals for customers, our shareholders, and Amazon.”

In the letter, Jassy also reminisced on guiding Amazon through “one of the harder macroeconomic years in recent memory,” a period that saw the e-commerce behemoth axe 27,000 positions as part of a big effort to cut expenses.

In the letter, Jassy stated that there were “an unusual number of concurrent challenges this past year” before detailing the actions that Amazon took to reconsider some of its free delivery options, give up on some of its physical store concepts, and drastically reduce overall staff.
Jassy’s compensation package was worth about $1.3 million, according to information released by Amazon in a securities filing on Thursday, and the CEO did not earn any additional stock awards in 2022. (Jassy received a pay package in 2021 that was primarily made up of stock awards and valued his overall salary at about $212 million.))

Jassy wrote in his letter that despite the difficulties at Amazon, he is “optimistic and energized by what lies ahead.”

snippet.title: "Uncertain Economy": Amazon To Lay Off Over 18,000 Employees
snippet.description:
Amazon announced Wednesday it will cut more than 18,000 jobs from its workforce, citing "the uncertain economy" and the fact that the online retail giant had "hired rapidly" during the pandemic. "Between the reductions we made in November and the ones we're sharing today, we plan to eliminate just over 18,000 roles," said CEO Andy Jassy in a statement to his staff. The company had announced 10,000 layoffs in November.

snippet.title: Lay-Offs Continue Into 2023, Amazon Cuts Over 18,000 Jobs, Salesforce To Cut 10% Of Its Workforce
snippet.description:
Amazon reportedly planned to lay off roughly 10,000 staffers last year, but the company chose to cut thousands of additional employees this month, bringing the total to over 18,000, CEO Andy Jassy said in a message to staff that cited an “uncertain economy”.

Meanwhile, Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff announced plans to cut 10% of its workforce “in the coming weeks” (estimated to affect roughly 7,900 of its 79,000 employees, according to the company) in an email to staff, saying the decision comes amid a “challenging” environment and as customers take a “more measured approach to their purchasing decisions.”

snippet.title: Insider Insights: Jim Cramer's Exclusive Interview with Amazon's CEO
snippet.description:
#amazonstockanalysis #amznstockearnings #amznstocktoday #jimcramer

In this exclusive video, Jim Cramer talks to Amazon's CEO, Andy Jassy. They discuss the stock's recent rally and Jassy's outlook for the company in the future.

Amazon CEO Andy Jassy talks about the value proposition of Prime, the company's membership program. Jassy explains that for $14.99 a month, members get fast, reliable, and unlimited free shipping on millions of items, access to exclusive events like Prime Day, and a variety of other discounts and deals. He also discusses the Prime video feature, which offers an exclusive selection of TV shows and movies. 

Jassy mentions that the program has been adding benefits to Prime for many years and that customers love the value they receive. He also discusses some of the special events that are available to Prime members. Next, Jassy talks about Amazon Web Services (AWS), which he says is a phenomenally profitable part of the company. Jassy notes that the rate of growth of AWS has slowed, but he thinks this is due to a slowdown in the overall economy rather than a lack of value in AWS. 

He explains that AWS was a major part of the decision-making during the pandemic, where the company chose to help customers weather the uncertainty instead of trying to squeeze every dollar from them, even though it could have led to lower revenue growth in the short term. Jassy is bullish about the future of AWS, with a focus on building a long-term business that helps customers and will ride along with them.

snippet.title: Jeff Bezos Steps Down as CEO of Amazon; Andy Jassy Becomes New CEO #InformationAI
snippet.description:
Go through my second channel :https://www.youtube.com/@max_world2007./about

In a surprising announcement in February 2021, Jeff Bezos, the founder and CEO of Amazon, revealed that he would be stepping down as CEO later in the year and transitioning to the role of Executive Chairman. This marks a significant change in leadership for the company, which Bezos has led as CEO for over two decades.

The announcement came as a shock to many, as Bezos has been synonymous with Amazon since he founded the company in 1994. Over the years, he has grown Amazon from an online bookseller to one of the world's largest and most valuable companies, with a market capitalization of over $1 trillion.

Bezos' decision to step down as CEO is not entirely unexpected, however. In recent years, he has taken on more of a high-level strategic role at Amazon, delegating day-to-day operations to senior executives. This move will allow him to focus more on these strategic initiatives, while still maintaining a leadership position at the company.

In his new role as Executive Chairman, Bezos will still be involved in Amazon's major decisions, but he will no longer be responsible for the company's day-to-day operations. Instead, that responsibility will fall to Andy Jassy, who has been with Amazon since 1997 and currently leads the company's profitable cloud computing division, Amazon Web Services (AWS).

Jassy is a well-respected leader within Amazon, and he has been instrumental in building AWS into the powerhouse it is today. Under his leadership, AWS has become the market leader in cloud computing, with a 32% share of the market, according to Synergy Research Group.

Jassy's appointment as CEO of Amazon has been met with praise from many in the business world. He is seen as a highly capable leader who understands the company's culture and values, and who is well-equipped to continue Amazon's growth trajectory.
